Indian football in 2025 continues to reel under a dual crisis — governance failures and on-field disappointments, with administrative issues often overshadowing match performances. A stark indicator of the disorder surfaced this year: while the Supreme Court held 16 hearings related to football, the men’s national team played only 12 matches, highlighting the extent of off-field chaos impacting the sport.

National Team Struggles

India suffered a historic setback with a defeat to Bangladesh, their first loss in 22 years against their South Asian rivals. Head coach Khalid Jamil faced scrutiny after the team’s lackluster performance, which left fans disillusioned.

While nations such as Haiti and Curaçao made strides on the global stage, India continued its cycle of underwhelming performances, raising concerns about long-term competitiveness.

On-Field Performance Concerns

India’s matches exposed glaring weaknesses:

  • Sluggish player movement
  • Lack of tactical cohesion
  • Poor preparation and execution

These issues reignited debates around player development, coaching standards, and long-term planning within Indian football.

Administrative Chaos in AIFF

The All India Football Federation (AIFF) remains embroiled in controversy and inefficiency, struggling to secure investment and manage domestic competitions.

A senior executive openly criticized the federation’s “laziness”, pointing to internal politics, bureaucratic hurdles, and a lack of professional leadership—hindrances that continue to stall progress across all levels of the sport.

ISL Revenue Disputes

For the last 15 years, AIFF has received ₹50 crore annually from the Football Sports Development Limited (FSDL), a Reliance subsidiary. With the contract expiring on December 8, 2025, negotiations for renewal have sparked conflict.

AIFF’s new demands include:

  • ₹37.5 crore annually, or
  • 5% of annual revenue

The federation’s valuation of other football properties, including the national teams, at just ₹12.5 crore, has raised eyebrows across the Indian football ecosystem.

Domestic Football in Coma

With investment declining and administrative instability, India’s domestic football structure is crumbling:

  • Clubs face severe financial stress
  • The Indian Super League’s long-term future is uncertain
  • Grassroots development lacks direction
  • Administrative mismanagement continues to erode progress

This stagnation threatens both elite and grassroots football, making it harder for India to produce competitive players.

Implications for Indian Football

  • Declining international competitiveness
  • Investor reluctance due to governance failures
  • Stunted youth development pathways
  • Reduced fan engagement and media coverage

Conclusion

Indian football in 2025 stands at a critical crossroads. A combination of weak governance, administrative paralysis, and poor on-field performances has created a deep crisis. Without urgent reforms, stronger leadership, and a clear strategic vision, the sport risks slipping further behind global and regional competitors.

Reviving the national team and rebuilding domestic leagues are essential steps to ensure sustainable long-term growth.
